schools.summary: Gill: some were children by special grace of adoption, all by national adoption — a peculiar privilege laying them under obligation to honour and obey God; they were not to conform to the Gentiles' extravagant mourning for the dead.

themes: Ye are the children of the LORD your God: ye shall not cut yourselves... for the dead
holiness rooted in sonship
the sons of God sorrowing not as the hopeless (1 Thess. 4:13)

schools.summary: Gill: set apart from all other people and devoted to God's worship, many sanctified in a special spiritual sense, and so should not conform to the customs of the Gentiles.
Keil & Delitzsch: a peculiar people unto God.

themes: thou art an holy people... the LORD hath chosen thee to be a peculiar people unto himself
election the ground of separation
a peculiar people distinct from the world (1 Pet. 2:9)

schools.summary: Gill: the abominable is so either in its own nature or because forbidden by the Lord, as declared in the following verses.
Ellicott: the distinctions between His creatures were alike established and removed by the Creator.

themes: Thou shalt not eat any abominable thing
the distinction resting on God's word
established and removed by the same Creator (Acts 10:15)

schools.summary: Gill: these were the beasts they might lawfully eat, allowed for their food, though not obliged to eat if they chose not.
Keil & Delitzsch: add the hart, roebuck, and the rest of the clean kinds.

themes: These are the beasts which ye shall eat: the ox, the sheep, and the goat
the clean beasts allowed, a liberty within limits
God's gifts enjoyed within his command

schools.summary: Gill: these were of the deer kind, agreeable food; harts were common in Canaan, and the roebuck and fallow deer were accounted delicious.

themes: The hart, and the roebuck, and the fallow deer, and the wild goat...
the clean game permitted
God's bounty within bounds, richly provided

schools.summary: Gill: two general rules — a beast that both parted the hoof and chewed the cud might be eaten, but one that did only the one, as the camel, hare, or coney, might not.

themes: every beast that parteth the hoof... and cheweth the cud... ye shall eat
the double mark of the clean
read as a parable of upright walk and meditation

schools.summary: Gill: the fishes that have fins and scales might be eaten, but those that have not were not to be eaten.

themes: These ye shall eat of all that are in the waters: all that have fins and scales
the clear mark of the clean fish
a figure of resisting the world and being guarded

schools.summary: Gill: the clean birds, by the Targum, are those with a craw, a naked crop, a superfluous talon, and not rapacious; only the unclean are named, so all others might be reckoned clean; Maimonides observes only the unclean are numbered.

themes: Of all clean birds ye shall eat
only the unclean named by exception
God's gifts outrunning his prohibitions

schools.summary: Gill: Jarchi observes the unclean birds are particularly mentioned to teach that the clean are more than the unclean; these are the same names as in Leviticus, except the glede.

themes: these are they of which ye shall not eat: the eagle, and the ossifrage, and the ospray
the unclean birds of prey forbidden
a figure of abhorring rapine and corruption

schools.summary: Clarke: daah is improperly translated vulture in Leviticus, meaning a kite or glede; daiyah here means a different bird, properly the vulture.
Ellicott: compares the list with Leviticus 11.

themes: the glede, and the kite, and the vulture after his kind
the exactness of the law marking every kind
the abhorrence of prey and death pressed again

schools.summary: Gill: the Targum reckons all flies, wasps or hornets, and worms that fly as birds among the unclean; they were not to be eaten.

themes: every creeping thing that flieth is unclean unto you: they shall not be eaten
the law reaching to the least creatures
a careful holiness in the smallest things

schools.summary: Gill: of all fowls but those excepted they might eat; Aben Ezra instances the clean locust, and the Targum allows every clean locust.

themes: But of all clean fowls ye may eat
the breadth of liberty within the law
God's bounty exceeding his bans

schools.summary: Gill: the law against eating what dies of itself is repeated from Leviticus.
Clarke: Calmet thinks the seething of a kid in its mother's milk refers to the paschal lamb, others to heathen custom.
Ellicott: treats the kid-in-milk prohibition likewise.

themes: Ye shall not eat of any thing that dieth of itself... Thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk
the food-laws grounded in holiness
reverence and tenderness in the home

schools.summary: Gill: this was a different tithe from that given to the Levites; this the owners themselves ate of, the tithing left to be made faithfully by them, of all the increase, year by year.

themes: Thou shalt truly tithe all the increase of thy seed... year by year
a faithful, exact rendering to God
the harvest his gift confessed (Prov. 3:9)

schools.summary: Gill: this second tithe was eaten by the owners before the Lord at the chosen place.
Keil & Delitzsch: that thou mayest learn to fear the LORD thy God always.

themes: thou shalt eat before the LORD... that thou mayest learn to fear the LORD thy God always
the sacred meal a school of reverence
joy in his gifts and awe of his presence meeting

schools.summary: Gill: this was for the Israelite whose dwelling was far from the place. Calvin: weighing whether this refers to tithes or firstfruits, notes the redemption-rule with its added fifth (Lev. 27:31) is here omitted.

themes: if the way be too long for thee, so that thou art not able to carry it
gracious provision for distance
the duty kept while the manner is eased

schools.summary: Gill: the tithe was turned into money, which would be lighter and easier of carriage.
Keil & Delitzsch: 'give it up for silver,' binding the silver in the hand and going to the place.

themes: Then shalt thou turn it into money, and bind up the money in thine hand
the worship kept whole, the burden lightened
God making his service practicable

schools.summary: Gill: he might buy what provision he would, what he best liked and was most agreeable to his appetite.
Keil & Delitzsch: the buying of oxen, sheep, wine, or strong drink, whatsoever the soul desired.

themes: thou shalt bestow that money for whatsoever thy soul lusteth after... and thou shalt rejoice
the festival tithe sanctifying gladness
true religion the sanctifier of joy (Phil. 4:4)

schools.summary: Gill, with Jarchi: as he was not to be forsaken in the first tithe, so neither here; he was not to be left behind but taken to share the entertainment, having no part nor inheritance.

themes: the Levite that is within thy gates; thou shalt not forsake him
the festal joy shared, not selfish
plenty given that we may share it

schools.summary: Gill: according to Aben Ezra a third tithe; the Jewish writers and Tobit speak variously, but it agrees best with the care of the stranger, fatherless, and widow that follows, laid up within their gates the same year.

themes: At the end of three years thou shalt bring forth all the tithe... and shalt lay it up within thy gates
the third-year tithe for the poor at home
worship and mercy bound together

schools.summary: Gill: the Levite, having no inheritance, was to share this poor's tithe, with the stranger, fatherless, and widow within their gates; the end was that the LORD might bless them in all the work of their hand.

themes: the stranger, and the fatherless, and the widow... shall eat and be satisfied; that the LORD thy God may bless thee
mercy to the poor lent to the LORD
the open hand blessed (Prov. 19:17)
